plant.description:
Botanical Family: (Note: The exact scientific botanical family name, Rutaceae, is not explicitly mentioned in the provided sources, but the sources universally group them together under the citrus plant classification).
2. Brief Description and Common Forms
Description: Citrus fruits are fleshy, acidic fruits that include varieties such as oranges, lemons, limes, grapefruits, tangerines, and pomelos1.... The anatomy of these fruits and their host plants naturally contain varying amounts of citric acid and distinct essential oils12.
Common Forms: In household environments, they are most commonly encountered as fresh whole fruits (which include the edible flesh, peels/rinds, stems, and leaves), as well as in the form of extracted juices and concentrated essential oils2....

toxins.name: Citrus Essential Oils (including D-limonene)
toxins.description:
in cats: Citrus essential oils are highly irritating to a cat's gastrointestinal tract, skin, and respiratory system1.... The intense, acidic smell alone is highly stimulating and can cause damage to a cat's sensitive respiratory tract3. If ingested or absorbed, these compounds act systemically to cause depression of the central nervous system, leading to a dangerous decrease in normal neurological functions14. Contact with the oils can also trigger severe skin allergies and topical poisoning reactions23.
Any notes on concentration or potency: These toxic oils are most highly concentrated in the peels, rinds, and stems of citrus fruits13. They are exceptionally potent and dangerous when extracted into pure, undiluted (neat) essential oils or concentrated juices56. Because of this high concentration, pet parents must be extremely careful to keep cats away from commercial products containing citrus extracts
toxins.concentration_notes: or potency: These toxic oils are most highly concentrated in the peels, rinds, and stems of citrus fruits13. They are exceptionally potent and dangerous when extracted into pure, undiluted (neat) essential oils or concentrated juices56. Because of this high concentration, pet parents must be extremely careful to keep cats away from commercial products containing citrus extracts

toxins.name: Citric Acid
toxins.description:
in cats: Citric acid functions primarily as a gastrointestinal irritant in felines. When ingested, the acid irritates the stomach lining, resulting in gastrointestinal upset, vomiting, and diarrhea14. If a cat consumes the acid in large, excessive amounts, it can also cross over to cause depression of the central nervous system4.
Any notes on concentration or potency: Unlike the essential oils, citric acid is naturally found in varying amounts throughout the fleshy, edible parts of citrus fruits like oranges, lemons, and limes14. The toxicity is dose-dependent: a small dose (such as a cat taking a bite of the fruit's flesh) will typically only result in mild stomach upset and irritation, whereas ingesting large amounts significantly increases the risk of severe neurological depression
toxins.concentration_notes: or potency: Unlike the essential oils, citric acid is naturally found in varying amounts throughout the fleshy, edible parts of citrus fruits like oranges, lemons, and limes14. The toxicity is dose-dependent: a small dose (such as a cat taking a bite of the fruit's flesh) will typically only result in mild stomach upset and irritation, whereas ingesting large amounts significantly increases the risk of severe neurological depression

symptoms.name: 1. Vomiting, Diarrhea, and Stomach Upset
symptoms.onset: Shortly after ingestion (exact timeframe is not specified in the sources,
symptoms.notes: The severity depends heavily on the amount and part of the fruit consumed. A small dose, such as a cat eating a piece of the fruit's flesh, usually results in mild stomach upset due to the citric acid12. However, ingestion of the peels—which house the toxic essential oils—or eating a large amount of the fruit can cause severe gastrointestinal distress

symptoms.name: Central Nervous System (CNS) Depression
symptoms.onset: Variable (develops as the toxins are absorbed systemically).
symptoms.notes: This dangerous decrease in normal neurological functions generally occurs if a cat ingests a significantly large amount of citrus fruit or is exposed to the highly concentrated essential oils found within the peels

symptoms.name: Skin Irritation and Allergic Reactions
symptoms.notes: The toxic compounds and essential oils (such as D-limonene) are heavily concentrated in the rind and peel of citrus fruits. Direct physical contact with the peels or extracted oils can trigger topical poisoning, skin allergies, and irritation

symptoms.name: Respiratory Tract Irritation
symptoms.notes: The intense, acidic scent of citrus fruits is extremely stimulating to felines. Smelling or inhaling these strong aromas and vaporized essential oils can irritate and potentially damage a cat's sensitive respiratory tract

treatments.name: Based on the sources, there is no specific antidote for citrus toxicity in cats1. Because the citric acid and concentrated essential oils found in citrus fruits
Induction of Emesis (Early Gastrointestinal Decontamination)
treatments.description:
of the procedure: If the citrus ingestion is caught very early (typically within 1 to 2 hours) and the cat is still asymptomatic, a veterinarian will administer a feline-specific α
2
	​

-adrenergic agonist, such as dexmedetomidine, to safely induce vomiting and expel the fruit or peel from the stomach before the toxins are absorbed3.
Any situation-specific
treatments.notes: Pet owners must never attempt to induce vomiting at home5. Using hydrogen peroxide to induce vomiting is strictly contraindicated in felines, as it can cause fatal hemorrhagic gastritis

treatments.name: Administration of Activated Charcoal (Secondary Decontamination)
treatments.description:
of the procedure: Following successful emesis, the veterinarian may administer oral activated charcoal to bind to and adsorb any residual toxins remaining in the stomach and gastrointestinal tract3.
Any situation-specific
treatments.notes: This helps trap the highly concentrated and toxic essential oils (found in citrus peels) in the gut, preventing them from entering the bloodstream and causing systemic neurological depression

treatments.name: Intravenous (IV) Fluid Therapy and Supportive Care
treatments.description:
of the procedure: The cat is hospitalized to receive continuous IV fluids (a drip) and undergo blood tests to closely monitor organ function while the body clears the toxins2.
Any situation-specific
treatments.notes: Because citrus ingestion can cause severe stomach upset, vomiting, and diarrhea, IV fluids are a critical supportive measure to prevent rapid dehydration, correct electrolyte imbalances, and support the cat's systemic recovery from CNS depression
